speaker: interviewer
text: For an interview with Jean Litz on August 8, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mom and dad when they got engaged.

speaker: interviewee
text: Icek and Eva Kozak. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My mother was 17 and my father was 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jean, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when I was a little girl. I don't remember how old I was. I think I was maybe six or seven. So my mother, and she-- her arm is around my brother Kalman, the one that passed away. My brother Reuben, he lives in Florida, my brother Morris, my dad, and my sister Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken in Mlynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the grave, a mass grave where they killed close to 2,000 Jews from my home town Mlynow. Maybe we have 30, 35 survivors out of the 2,000 men, women, and children. That was-- they put a monument when the Russians first came in. But now, what I understand, there's not even a monument, nothing. There's no mark, there's nothing left, nothing left.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in Camp [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if it was 1945 or '46. But these clothes I'm wearing, the dresses, was sent by [INAUDIBLE] from a care package. And we were dressed pretty good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Beautiful, for there was beautiful here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's me. The first Christmas in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were here about six months at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me in the first, in the summer of-- when I worked in the Catskills [BACKROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] I forgot all of that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here you are at a party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I am at a party with my mother and my father, me and my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was my brother-in-law's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] birthday-- or I don't remember really.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my mother and father's 70th anniversary-- 7-0. Yeah, they-- my mother lived another two months after this picture was taken. She died-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1960-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what is this, 1990? What am I talking about? 1996-- she died in 1991.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years later, three years later, yeah. What?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, my daughter Miriam, Miriam Millan. She's a wonderful daughter. She's the only one I have. I only have one child.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let's see-- 23 years ago. I don't remember the date, I can't think of the date right now. Isn't that something?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your pride and joy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my pride and joy. That's my granddaughter, my only one, Lisa. She is a-- she is a wonderful girl. She goes to the University of Maryland. And she graduates in May. And I really have something to look forward to.

speaker: interviewee
text: These earrings are, are mine-- my mother's before me, and my grandmother's. They belong to my granddaughter now. That's fifth generation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- this is the only thing I left home with, were my earrings. And that my mother took a scissors, that was it. And I hope it's going to go to my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] next generation, to the children of my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jean, you're here with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my husband, Marvin. Everybody calls him Sonny. This is my daughter Miriam, and this is my granddaughter Lisa. And I'll let Sonny say a few words, if you want to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you've met my family, my daughter, my granddaughter, and my wife, who's been through a lot. And thank God that she's-- she was able to come through it and be able to pass the story on to others, to confirm what has happened to people like her in the past and so that it can be remembered and documented.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also am very happy as a child of a Holocaust survivor to finally have my mother's account of her survival and her ordeal, what she went through to be legitimized in this documentation. And I only hope that all who view it in the future, especially the young people, realize the sense of responsibility that they have in the future to keep this from ever happening again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know what to say. I don't know, I guess [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being part of the younger generation, I didn't grow up in, in the house with my grandmother. But I think that because of what she went through, a lot of values were instilled then upon my mother and upon me. And, you know, I think it's made us all, you know, what we are, what she went through, and, and you know, I can't fathom it. But hearing the stories, you know, just passed down, you know, to me they're only stories. But I know that, you know, they're real in her mind, which is, you know, scary and but true. So, and I fully intend to pass it down to my children and their children after that. And I think that's what's really important, so.
